text:
NUCLEAR MEDICINE / RADIOLOGY
PET/CT WITH FDG — WHOLE BODY

INDICATION: 52-year-old female with HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition), treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation (70 Gy IMRT in 35 fractions, concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 x2 cycles). Study requested approximately 12 weeks following completion of chemoradiation for response assessment. Prior staging PET/CT available for comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Following intravenous administration of approximately 12.4 mCi of F-18 fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG), the patient rested for approximately 60 minutes in a quiet, darkened room. Whole-body PET emission imaging was performed from the vertex to the mid-thighs. Low-dose CT was obtained for attenuation correction and anatomic localization. Patient blood glucose at time of injection: 92 mg/dL. No intravenous contrast was administered for this examination. Images were reconstructed using iterative technique and reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes, as well as maximum intensity projection (MIP).

COMPARISON: Prior staging FDG PET/CT.

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK — PRIMARY SITE:
At the right base of tongue, there is no longer any focal hypermetabolic activity to suggest residual viable tumor. On the prior study, there was a prominent FDG-avid mass at this site with an SUVmax of 12.4. At present, the region demonstrates only faint, diffuse, symmetric FDG uptake indistinguishable from background oropharyngeal mucosal activity (SUVmax 1.8 at the site of the previously defined primary). A small residual area of soft tissue thickening is appreciated on the low-dose CT component, but this is without associated hypermetabolism and is consistent with post-radiation fibrotic/inflammatory change. No discrete mass or focal nodule is identified at the right base of tongue or right glossoepiglottic fold.

HEAD AND NECK — NODAL STATIONS:
The previously FDG-avid right level IIA lymph node (SUVmax 8.7, measuring 2.4 cm on prior study) is no longer hypermetabolic. A small residual soft tissue density measuring approximately 0.9 cm is present in the right level IIA region on the CT component, but demonstrates no FDG activity above background (SUVmax 1.5). This appearance is consistent with a successfully treated, fibrosed lymph node. No pathologically enlarged or FDG-avid lymph nodes are identified in the right or left neck at any station, including levels I through V, the posterior cervical chains, retropharyngeal regions, or supraclavicular fossae bilaterally.

OROPHARYNX / ORAL CAVITY / LARYNX:
There is mildly increased, diffuse, symmetric FDG uptake throughout the oropharyngeal mucosa bilaterally, including the soft palate, anterior tonsillar pillars, and posterior pharyngeal wall. This pattern of diffuse mucosal hypermetabolism is characteristic of post-radiation inflammatory change and is not specific for residual neoplasm. No focal or asymmetric uptake is identified. The nasopharynx and hypopharynx are unremarkable. The larynx demonstrates symmetric, low-grade FDG activity without any focal lesion. The salivary glands are somewhat diminished in metabolic activity bilaterally, consistent with post-radiation effect.

CHEST:
The lungs are clear bilaterally. No focal pulmonary consolidation, mass, or nodule with abnormal FDG activity is identified. There are no FDG-avid mediastinal, hilar, or axillary lymph nodes. The mediastinal structures and cardiac silhouette appear unremarkable on the low-dose CT. No pleural effusion.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:
The liver demonstrates normal homogeneous FDG uptake. No focal hepatic lesion is identified. The spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys appear unremarkable without focal FDG-avid abnormality. Normal physiologic FDG activity is noted in the kidneys, ureters, and urinary bladder. No pathologically enlarged mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymph nodes. No ascites or pelvic mass.

SKELETAL SURVEY:
No focal osseous lesion with abnormal FDG uptake to suggest metastatic involvement. Degenerative changes are noted in the thoracic and lumbar spine, without hypermetabolic activity.

OTHER:
A percutaneous gastrostomy tube (PEG) is noted in situ, with its tip projecting over the gastric body. No peritubular soft tissue uptake to suggest complication. Normal physiologic FDG activity in the gastrointestinal tract.

IMPRESSION:

1. COMPLETE METABOLIC RESPONSE at the right base of tongue primary site. Previously identified hypermetabolic right base of tongue mass (SUVmax 12.4) is no longer present. Only minimal residual soft tissue thickening without FDG avidity is seen, consistent with post-radiation fibrosis and/or inflammation rather than viable tumor.

2. COMPLETE METABOLIC RESPONSE in the previously involved right level IIA cervical lymph node. The node has markedly decreased in size and shows no residual hypermetabolic activity.

3. No FDG-avid cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally.

4. Mildly increased diffuse oropharyngeal mucosal FDG uptake consistent with expected post-radiation inflammatory change; no focal or asymmetric activity to suggest nodal or mucosal recurrence.

5. No evidence of distant metastatic disease in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.

Overall, the findings are consistent with a favorable treatment response following definitive chemoradiation for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. No residual hypermetabolic disease identified.

Dictated by: Attending Radiologist / Nuclear Medicine Physician
Transcribed and verified electronically.
